metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that `mcp_verify` declares `needs: publish` (inheriting the current mcp_registry dependency chain) so the PyPI readiness wait and registry publish ordering are preserved after the job split.
findings.finding: The plan splits the existing `mcp_registry` job (which has `needs: publish` at release.yml line 111) into `mcp_verify` + `mcp_registry`, but specifies only `mcp_registry needs: mcp_verify` — it never assigns the `needs: publish` dependency to either new job. If `mcp_verify` starts in parallel with `publish` (no `needs: publish`), the 'Wait for PyPI' logic and the registry's package-ownership validation against the live PyPI release will race against an unpublished version, breaking the release flow that the dependency chain currently enforces.
findings.location: What section — 'Security contract — structural job split'; Scope — 'mcp_verify … mcp_registry'; AC #5
findings.suggested_fix: Add `needs: publish` to the new `mcp_verify` job definition: `mcp_verify: needs: publish`. Then `mcp_registry: needs: mcp_verify` naturally inherits the full chain (publish → mcp_verify → mcp_registry).

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the Scope section's description of test_release_workflow_pins.py check (f) with the What section and AC by adding 'and the write commands that populate each fragment' to the Scope bullet, so the three descriptions are consistent.
findings.finding: The Scope section's description of test_release_workflow_pins.py check (f) says it verifies only that 'the three fragment names appear in release.yml and a consolidation job downloads + concatenates them', omitting the requirement — stated explicitly in both the What section and the AC — that the test must also assert the *write commands that populate each fragment* (e.g. the mcp step redirecting version+digest into evidence-mcp) so that an empty-file implementation fails. An implementer working from the Scope section would write a weaker test that the AC would then silently contradict.
findings.location: Scope section — tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py description of check (f) vs What section 'Print and retain release evidence' paragraph and AC 'release-evidence pipeline' item
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section under tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py, change the check (f) bullet to read: '(f) the release-evidence wiring — the three fragment names this story delivers (evidence-shapin|evidence-build|evidence-mcp) appear in release.yml **with their write commands** (e.g. the mcp step redirecting version+digest into evidence-mcp, so an empty-file implementation fails), and a consolidation job downloads + concatenates them.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the plan where the actionlint version pin and SHA-256 are recorded (e.g., `ACTIONLINT_VERSION` / `ACTIONLINT_SHA256` variables at the top of the Makefile, mirroring the MCP publisher pattern), and add a one-line update-process note to the 'Pinning' section of `docs/releasing.md` alongside the MCP publisher update instructions.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `actionlint` is installed via 'a pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite' but never states (a) what version to pin, (b) where in the repo the pinned version string and SHA-256 are recorded, or (c) how a future maintainer updates the pin. This is an E2 defaultable gap: the executor knows the mechanism but must independently decide on version/SHA storage without plan guidance.
findings.location: Scope — 'Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev]' bullet; 'actionlint is a Go binary — install it via a pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Makefile scope bullet: 'Store the pin as `ACTIONLINT_VERSION ?= <version>` and `ACTIONLINT_SHA256 ?= <hex>` at the top of the Makefile; document the update procedure (download new release, recompute SHA-256, update both variables) in the `docs/releasing.md` Pinning section alongside the MCP publisher update instructions.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify actionlint's single-file scoping: run `actionlint --help` or consult docs to confirm `actionlint .github/workflows/release.yml` limits findings to that file only, and record the confirmed release URL template (e.g., `https://github.com/rhysd/actionlint/releases/download/v{VERSION}/actionlint_{VERSION}_linux_amd64.tar.gz`) and a pinned version+SHA-256 in the plan.
findings.finding: `actionlint` is introduced as a new binary dependency installed via a 'pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite,' but the plan names no specific version, no release URL template, and no SHA-256 — making the integration Unverified. Additionally, the plan asserts `actionlint .github/workflows/release.yml` scopes output to that single file, but this specific CLI surface (single-file argument suppressing all other workflow findings) has not been probed and is Unverified. If actionlint does not support single-file scoping, the gate would emit pre-existing findings from other workflows.
findings.location: Scope section: 'actionlint installed via a **pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification** in a Makefile prerequisite'; AC3: 'actionlint .github/workflows/release.yml'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: (a) the confirmed actionlint release URL template (`https://github.com/rhysd/actionlint/releases/download/v{VERSION}/actionlint_{VERSION}_linux_amd64.tar.gz`), (b) a placeholder for the specific version and SHA-256 to be locked at implementation time, and (c) an explicit note that `actionlint` accepts individual workflow file paths and scopes its output accordingly (verified from `actionlint --help`). If single-file scoping is not supported, specify a `.actionlint.yml` ignore-pattern config file as the scope mechanism instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `pip-tools` to `pyproject.toml [dev]` with a concrete install path OR commit to `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` as the sole tool (removing `pip-compile` from the Scope, AC3, and docs grep substring), so the lock-file generation tool has exactly one install path and zero ambiguity.
findings.finding: pip-tools (`pip-compile`) is introduced as the sole canonical lock-file generation tool with zero existing call-sites in the codebase — absent from `pyproject.toml [dev]`, the Makefile, and every workflow — while the plan's own What section names `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` as a co-equal alternative. Hard-coding `pip-compile` in the Scope, AC3, and the docs substring grep (`pip-compile --generate-hashes`) introduces a new zero-call-site external tool dependency when an alternative that may already be present in the developer's environment is available. The Rule-of-Three test fails: there is no prior `pip-compile` usage anywhere in the repo.
findings.location: Scope section (.github/release-requirements.txt), AC3, docs AC (literal grep substring 'pip-compile --generate-hashes')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the docs grep literal with a tool-agnostic substring like `--generate-hashes` (present in both `pip-compile` and `uv pip compile` output), and commit to whichever tool the project already has or wants to adopt — add it to `pyproject.toml [dev]` or `Makefile` with an explicit install step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding `make test` or dedicated CI step) that actually runs `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt` in a fresh venv and executes `python -m build --no-isolation` (sdist/wheel, no publish) against the repo, so the hash-locked build path is exercised end-to-end before merge.
findings.finding: The `--no-isolation` + hash-locked build path is only statically verified (test_release_workflow_pins.py checks the flag is present in release.yml) but is never actually executed in CI. A malformed `.github/release-requirements.txt` (wrong hash, missing transitive dep, pip-compile artifact mismatch) or a runtime incompatibility with `--no-isolation` would only surface during an actual release run — not under `make lint && make typecheck && make test`. E6(f) requires that a cutover to a new production code path have at least one AC that exercises it end-to-end; no such AC exists here.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Hatchling's version is enforced end-to-end via --no-isolation' and 'Release build tools install from .github/release-requirements.txt with --require-hashes'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'A `make build-smoke` target (or equivalent CI step) creates a fresh venv, runs `pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r .github/release-requirements.txt`, then `python -m build --no-isolation --outdir /tmp/dist`, and exits non-zero on any failure — run in the Gerrit Verified gate alongside `make test`.' Wire `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` into this smoke target so the parsed env-vs-lock guard is also exercised live.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--connect-timeout 30 --max-time 120` (or equivalent) to every `curl` invocation for external downloads (MCP publisher in `mcp_verify` and `actionlint` in the Makefile prerequisite) so network failures surface quickly rather than hanging the runner.
findings.finding: No connect-timeout or max-time is specified for the `curl` downloads of the MCP publisher (in `mcp_verify`) or `actionlint` (in the Makefile prerequisite). A slow or unresponsive GitHub Releases endpoint will cause the CI runner to hang silently until GitHub's 6-hour job timeout, wasting runner minutes and blocking the release pipeline with no actionable signal.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher download steps; Scope — Makefile prerequisite for actionlint
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `curl -fsSL` with `curl -fsSL --connect-timeout 30 --max-time 120` for both the MCP publisher and actionlint downloads; document that on non-zero exit the operator should re-trigger the job.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a version-mismatch fixture case to the `check_build_env_locked.py` unit test (e.g., lock pins `hatchling==1.2.3`, freeze shows `hatchling==1.2.4`) and assert the script exits non-zero, covering the distinct version-comparison branch alongside the existing 'extra package' failure case.
findings.finding: The test plan for `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` only explicitly covers 'fails on an extra package' (a package present in the env but absent from the lock), but omits a test for the version-mismatch failure mode (a package present in both the env and the lock but at different versions). Version substitution — where a supply-chain attacker replaces `hatchling==1.2.3` with `hatchling==1.2.3.evil` — is the exact scenario the script is designed to catch, and it is a distinct code path from the 'absent from lock' branch.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 / `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` test description; Scope — `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py`
findings.suggested_fix: In the test fixture, create a lock entry `hatchling==1.2.3 --hash=sha256:abc...` and a freeze entry `hatchling==1.2.4`, call `check_build_env_locked.py` with those fixtures, and assert `returncode != 0`. This is a third fixture case alongside the existing clean-pass and extra-package-fail cases.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the scope exclusion wording from 'already SHA-pinned' to the accurate rationale — e.g., 'pinning non-release workflows is deferred to a follow-up (they currently contain unpinned actions; this story is bounded to release.yml)'.
findings.finding: The Scope exclusion 'Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned' rests on a false premise: non-release workflows are NOT already SHA-pinned. A grep of the repo finds unpinned `actions/checkout@v7` in test.yml (5 occurrences), _optionality.yml (4), mirror-guard.yml, external-integration.yml (2), codeql.yml, verify-identity.yaml, eval-plan-review-container.yml (2), terraform-drift.yml, reconcile-bridge-canary.yml, eval.yml (2), reconcile-bridge.yml; and unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6` in all the same files plus gerrit-verify.yaml:75. The exclusion clause 'already SHA-pinned' is directly refuted by these grep results.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned' to 'Out of scope: pinning other workflows (test.yml, _optionality.yml, gerrit-verify.yaml, etc.) — all have unpinned actions/checkout@v7 and actions/setup-python@v6, but fixing them is deferred to a follow-up story to keep this story bounded to release.yml.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate pip-tools as an edit target: either add `pip-tools` to `pyproject.toml [dev]` (so `pip install -e '.[dev]'` installs it) or add an explicit install step/target in the Makefile, and update the Scope section accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan names `pip-compile --generate-hashes` as the canonical lock-file generation command in AC3, the docs literal grep check, and the Scope section, but never identifies pip-tools as an edit target to add to any installable location (pyproject.toml [dev], Makefile, or a workflow). The plan treats pip-compile as an offline developer tool, but provides no documented install path for contributors who need to regenerate the lock.
findings.location: Scope section (.github/release-requirements.txt); AC3; Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev] edit targets
findings.suggested_fix: Add `pip-tools` to the `[dev]` extra in `pyproject.toml` and note this edit in the Scope section alongside the zizmor/actionlint additions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `sha256sum -c` re-verification of the extracted `mcp-publisher` binary (not just the archive) in `mcp_registry` immediately after `download-artifact` and before `chmod +x`, to close the artifact-handoff integrity gap and make the security contract mechanically true.
findings.finding: The plan's security contract ('the OIDC-privileged mcp_registry job never holds an unverified binary') is not mechanically closed: mcp_verify sha256sum-checks the *archive* (mcp-publisher.tar.gz), but the *extracted binary* is uploaded via actions/upload-artifact and re-downloaded in mcp_registry — and mcp_registry executes it without re-verifying its digest. GitHub Actions artifact storage provides no cryptographic integrity guarantee against the downloaded artifact matching what was uploaded. An attacker or storage corruption that substitutes the extracted binary after the archive check but before execution would go undetected.
findings.location: What section — 'Security contract — structural job split' / 'MCP publisher — download → verify → execute' / AC #5
findings.suggested_fix: In mcp_registry, after download-artifact, add: `echo "<pinned-sha256>  mcp-publisher" | sha256sum -c --strict` (the binary's SHA-256, precomputed from the extracted binary at pin time, stored as a workflow-level env var or constant alongside the archive SHA-256). This ensures the OIDC-privileged job verifies before executing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for choosing pip-tools over `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` (e.g. wider CI adoption, deterministic output format, or explicit exclusion of uv if its output format diverges), and if uv is actually preferred, update the Scope/AC/docs grep check to use the uv command consistently.
findings.finding: The plan commits pip-tools (`pip-compile --generate-hashes`) as the canonical lock-file generation tool throughout the Scope, all ACs, and the docs grep check literal (`grep -q 'pip-compile --generate-hashes'`), while only mentioning `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` as an unnamed parenthetical alternative ('or') with no resolution. The plan provides no positive rationale for choosing pip-tools over uv — which is already present in the ecosystem this project targets and would produce equivalent hash-locked output. Absent a stated rationale, an implementer cannot know whether the pip-tools choice is intentional or incidental.
findings.location: What section — 'Create a reviewed release lock/constraints file'; Scope — '.github/release-requirements.txt'; AC #3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` (single-file invocation) produces findings ONLY for `release.yml` and not for other files in `.github/workflows/`; document this in the plan, and specify a fallback scoping mechanism (e.g., `--config` with a per-workflow allow-list) if single-file suppression is incomplete.
findings.finding: The plan's entire scoping strategy for `zizmor` depends on `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` limiting findings to only `release.yml` and not emitting findings for other workflow files (e.g., `gerrit-verify.yaml:75`'s unpinned `setup-python@v6`). The plan explicitly states this scoping is why zizmor is invoked with an explicit path argument rather than repo-wide. This specific CLI behavior — that a file-path argument truly suppresses all findings from other workflow files, including those reachable via `needs:` or reusable-workflow references — is Unverified (not probed from `zizmor --help`).
findings.location: Scope section: 'zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml and actionlint .github/workflows/release.yml, NOT repo-wide — otherwise they would flag pre-existing out-of-scope issues in other workflows (e.g. gerrit-verify.yaml:75) and break the very Gerrit Verified gate that runs make lint'
findings.suggested_fix: Run `zizmor --help` and `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` locally (or in a scratch branch) to confirm the file-scoping behavior before committing to this approach. If zizmor does fully scope to the named file, add a one-line note confirming this to the plan. If it does not, replace the scoping strategy with a `zizmor.toml` config file that ignores all workflows except `release.yml`, or with a `--config` flag pointing to a per-invocation allow-list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `docs/releasing.md` Pinning section (or add a short ADR/CLAUDE.md entry) to document the `--no-isolation` architectural decision: why pip's isolated build is not used, why `pyproject.toml [build-system]` is intentionally unpinned, and that the hash-locked venv is the enforcement mechanism — and add an AC substring check (e.g. `--no-isolation`) to `test_release_workflow_pins.py`.
findings.finding: The architectural decision to use `--no-isolation` with a pre-installed hash-locked venv (rather than pip's default isolated build) is a significant, non-obvious build-system contract that is discussed only in the plan body, not in any living document (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or a `docs/releasing.md` section beyond the three narrow grep-checked substrings).
findings.location: What section: '--no-isolation' decision rationale; `docs/releasing.md` Pinning section AC; Scope: `pyproject.toml — no change to [build-system]`
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to `docs/releasing.md` Pinning section: 'The release build uses `python -m build --no-isolation` against a pre-installed hash-locked venv (`.github/release-requirements.txt`), making Hatchling's version enforcement entirely workflow-side; `pyproject.toml [build-system]` is intentionally left without a version pin.' Add `--no-isolation` to the AC substring checks.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the base allowlist (location, current entries, and criteria for extending it) in either a comment block at the top of `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` or the `docs/releasing.md` Pinning section, and note that additions require a reviewed change.
findings.finding: The base allowlist (`pip`, `setuptools`, `wheel`) in `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py` is hardcoded with no config mechanism and no documented procedure for extending it, yet it is a threshold expected to evolve (e.g. if `ubuntu-latest` starts pre-installing additional base packages).
findings.location: Scope: `scripts/check_build_env_locked.py`; What section: 'fails if any installed package is absent from the lock or version-mismatched, modulo a small documented base allowlist (pip, setuptools, wheel)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring to `check_build_env_locked.py` listing `BASE_ALLOWLIST = {'pip', 'setuptools', 'wheel'}` as an explicit named constant with a comment explaining why each entry is permitted, and reference this constant from the unit-test fixture so any change to the allowlist is immediately visible in the test diff.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete rationale for what `actionlint` catches in `release.yml` that `zizmor` does not (e.g., a specific expression-syntax or undefined-context check relevant to this story's ACs), or remove `actionlint` from this story's scope and defer it to the follow-up repo-wide adoption ticket.
findings.finding: `actionlint` is added to `make lint` alongside `zizmor`, but no AC item is attributed solely to `actionlint` — every stated enforcement goal (full-SHA pinning, persist-credentials, over-broad/OIDC permissions) is attributed to `zizmor`. The plan provides no positive rationale for what `actionlint` catches that `zizmor` misses for this story's done-definition. `actionlint` is a general YAML/expression-syntax linter for GitHub Actions, which is a good general practice, but its addition here reads as additive complexity without a grounded requirement gap.
findings.location: Scope section (Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev]), AC3 ('zizmor + actionlint run under make lint')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the release-evidence pipeline with the parent epic: either update the parent epic (2610-9557-c3c1-460c) to include evidence collection/retention in its Finding-4 success criterion before claiming this story, or remove the evidence-pipeline deliverables (fragment naming, consolidation job, operator-attestation instructions, check-(f) wiring assertions) from this leaf's scope — the parent's SC4 does not contain them.
findings.finding: The leaf introduces a release-evidence pipeline — distinctly-named job-scoped fragments (evidence-shapin, evidence-build, evidence-mcp), a consolidation job that concatenates them into release-evidence.txt, test assertions that check write-command content (not merely fragment-name presence), and an operator-attestation requirement directing the releaser to attach the artifact to the release ticket — as a first-class deliverable. This does not appear anywhere in the parent epic's Finding-4 success criterion or its ACs. The parent SC4 says only: 'Every executable the release depends on is identified by an immutable reference … and checkouts do not persist credentials.' The parent's ACs make no mention of evidence collection, retention, fragment naming, or operator attestation. The leaf steps outside the parent's contracted scope by adding a substantial evidence-recording subsystem.
findings.location: Leaf What section ('Print and retain release evidence…'); Leaf Scope (evidence fragment names, consolidation step); Leaf ACs ('The release-evidence pipeline is wired so each job writes its data to a distinctly-named fragment…')
findings.suggested_fix: Update the parent epic's Finding-4 success criterion to add: 'and each release job records its action SHA, tool versions, and publisher digest into a named evidence artifact, with a consolidation job producing release-evidence.txt.' Then re-review the leaf. Alternatively, if evidence is a separate concern, carve it into its own story under the epic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate `actionlint-py` (PyPI-published pip-installable wrapper for actionlint) as the installation path for actionlint — add it to `pyproject.toml [dev]` alongside `zizmor`, matching the project's existing `pip install -e '.[dev]'` pattern, before committing to the bespoke Makefile Go-binary download approach.
findings.finding: The plan installs `actionlint` via a bespoke Makefile prerequisite (pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification) — a custom Go-binary install mechanism — without considering or rejecting `actionlint-py`, the established PyPI-published pip-installable wrapper for actionlint that is maintained alongside the official binary. This bypasses the project's existing `pip install -e '.[dev]'` install path (which is exactly what the Gerrit gate uses) in favor of a novel ad-hoc pattern, when prior art provides a simpler one.
findings.location: Scope section — Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev] bullet; AC3 ('actionlint installed via a pinned-version release download with SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite')
findings.suggested_fix: Add `actionlint-py>=X.Y` to `pyproject.toml [dev]` (pinned exactly, consistent with how ruff and pytest-xdist are pinned). Remove the Makefile Go-binary download prerequisite for actionlint. This places actionlint on the same install path as every other dev tool and is maintained by the same upstream team.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document that `zizmor` ships binary wheels on PyPI for the linux/amd64 + Python 3.12 target used by the Gerrit CI runner (cite the PyPI project page or a test `pip install zizmor` result), and note the confirmed version in the plan before committing to `pyproject.toml [dev]`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'zizmor is pip-installable' as the basis for pinning it in `pyproject.toml [dev]` — an unverified capability claim that load-bears the entire AC3 enforcement path (the Gerrit gate installs via `pip install -e '.[dev]'`, so a missing or non-functional pip wheel would cause `make lint` to fail with 'command not found'). The plan cites no source confirming zizmor publishes pip-installable binary wheels for the Linux runner environment.
findings.location: Scope section — Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev] bullet; AC3 ('zizmor pinned in pyproject.toml [dev]')

findings.finding: The scope-exclusion 'Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned' is false as a codebase premise: `gerrit-verify.yaml:75` (in the `require-ticket` job) has an unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6`, and multiple other workflows (`test.yml`, `_optionality.yml`, `external-integration.yml`, `mirror-guard.yml`, `eval.yml`, `reconcile-bridge.yml`, `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml`) all use unpinned `actions/checkout@v7` and `actions/setup-python@v6`. The plan then adds `zizmor` to `make lint` with no specified scope restriction. Running `zizmor` over the entire `.github/workflows/` tree (the default) under `make lint` → Gerrit Verified gate would generate findings for all those workflows, breaking the gate. The plan says it touches 'only `release.yml`' but provides no `zizmor` config, `--only` flag, or `.zizmor.toml` scoping that confines zizmor to `release.yml` specifically.
findings.location: Why section ('gerrit-verify.yaml:75 still has an unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6`'); Scope section ('Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned'); AC3 ('`zizmor` + `actionlint` run under `make lint`')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Add a `.zizmor.toml` configuration file that limits `zizmor` to only `.github/workflows/release.yml` (or explicitly enumerate all workflows to include), so that unpinned actions in out-of-scope workflows (e.g., `gerrit-verify.yaml:75`, `test.yml`, `_optionality.yml`) do not cause Gerrit gate failures.' OR expand scope to pin all workflows.

findings.finding: `zizmor` added to `make lint` would scan ALL `.github/workflows/*.yml` files, not just `release.yml`, but 21+ other workflow files contain unpinned `actions/checkout@v7` and `actions/setup-python@v6` references (e.g. `test.yml:36`, `test.yml:37`, `gerrit-verify.yaml:75`, `_optionality.yml:55`, `mirror-guard.yml:36`, `external-integration.yml:35`, `eval.yml:45`, `codeql.yml:40`, etc.). The plan says fixing non-release workflows is 'out of scope' but does not specify that `zizmor` in `make lint` will be restricted to `release.yml` only. This means `make lint` (which is called by the Gerrit Verified gate via `gerrit-verify.yaml`) would immediately fail on every change submitted after this story lands, blocking the entire team's workflow until every other workflow is also pinned.
findings.location: Scope section / `Makefile` + `pyproject.toml [dev]` — add `zizmor` and `actionlint` to `make lint`; Out of scope clause: 'pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` (explicit path argument) to the Makefile `lint` target instead of `zizmor .github/workflows/` or a bare `zizmor`, so only `release.yml` is checked. Document this deliberate scoping decision in the Scope section with a note that other workflows will be pinned in a follow-on story.

findings.finding: The plan's scope-exclusion claim 'Out of scope: pinning non-release workflows already SHA-pinned' is factually false for the broader workflow corpus: `test.yml`, `mirror-guard.yml`, `external-integration.yml`, `_optionality.yml`, `reconcile-bridge.yml`, `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml`, `verify-identity.yaml`, `eval.yml`, `eval-plan-review-container.yml`, `terraform-drift.yml` all use mutable `actions/checkout@v7` and/or `actions/setup-python@v6` tags. If `zizmor` is added to `make lint` and scans all `.github/workflows/*.yml` files (its natural default scope), it will fail on these unpinned references and break the Gerrit Verified gate for every change — contradicting the plan's claim that only `release.yml` needs fixing.
findings.location: Out of scope section; Scope section (Makefile + shared lint config bullet); Why section ('the generic action-security checks are not yet enforced on release.yml')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, specify the exact zizmor invocation (e.g. `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml`) so it audits only the targeted file, consistent with the stated out-of-scope boundary.

findings.finding: Adding zizmor to `make lint` (global, runs on all workflows) while declaring `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6` out of scope creates a mechanism deadlock: zizmor will flag the out-of-scope unpinned action in gerrit-verify.yaml, causing `make lint` to fail, which will break the Gerrit Verified gate itself. The plan says zizmor enforces 'across `release.yml`' in some places but specifies no scoping configuration (e.g., a `.zizmor.yml` with per-file ignores, or a `--path` flag limiting zizmor to `release.yml` only) that would prevent it from flagging gerrit-verify.yaml. Without this, the plan's own Gerrit gate becomes unpassable on the day this story lands — before the gerrit-verify.yaml fix lands.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev] — add zizmor and actionlint to make lint'; Why section: 'Fixing gerrit-verify.yaml's own unpinned setup-python is out of scope'; gerrit-verify.yaml:75
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add a `.zizmor.yml` config with a `ignore` rule for `gerrit-verify.yaml`'s `setup-python` usage and document it in the Scope section, OR (b) expand the story scope to fix `gerrit-verify.yaml:75` at the same time (a one-line SHA-pin). Option (b) is simpler and eliminates the technical debt; option (a) allows the stated scope boundary to hold but requires a documented rationale comment in `.zizmor.yml`.

findings.finding: zizmor is added to `make lint` (which runs under the Gerrit Verified gate on ALL workflows), but gerrit-verify.yaml:75 has an unpinned `actions/setup-python@v6` that the plan explicitly declares out of scope to fix. If zizmor scans all workflow files in `.github/workflows/`, it will flag this unpinned reference and break `make lint` — blocking every Gerrit Verified vote. The AC language ('zizmor reports no findings on `release.yml`') implies zizmor is scoped to a single file, but neither the plan body nor any Scope item specifies a `--config`, path argument, or `zizmor release.yml` invocation that would limit the scan to release.yml only.
findings.location: Scope section ('Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev] — add zizmor and actionlint to make lint'), AC3 ('zizmor reports no findings on release.yml'), gerrit-verify.yaml:75
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope/Makefile bullet, write: `zizmor .github/workflows/release.yml` (scoped to the single file this story owns) rather than `zizmor` with no argument. Document that gerrit-verify.yaml's unpinned action is tracked separately and zizmor is intentionally scoped to release.yml for this story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Renumber the test_release_workflow_pins.py checks to start at (a) (or add a note explaining why the list begins at (c)), and ensure the label namespace is not shared with the docs/releasing.md AC sub-items to avoid cross-context confusion.
findings.finding: The test_release_workflow_pins.py check list in both the What and Scope sections begins at (c), skipping (a) and (b) with no explanation of whether earlier checks were removed, delegated to zizmor, or simply never existed. An implementer has no way to determine whether checks (a) and (b) are intentionally absent or accidentally dropped, which could cause either wasted investigation time or a real gap if they represent dropped requirements.
findings.location: What section — 'tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py (new)' paragraph; Scope section — corresponding test description
findings.suggested_fix: Either renumber the test checks (a)–(f) sequentially, or insert a parenthetical such as '(checks (a)/(b) are enforced by zizmor and therefore not in the unit test)' after the test description preamble in both What and Scope sections.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an explicit, ordered three-step download protocol for actionlint in the Scope section (matching the MCP publisher pattern: (1) `curl -fsSL <exact-url> -o actionlint.tar.gz`, (2) `echo '<pinned-sha256>  actionlint.tar.gz' | sha256sum -c --strict`, (3) extract and install) so the access posture of this public-internet download is a stated security contract, not an implied one.
findings.finding: The actionlint binary download — a public-internet boundary crossing — lacks an explicit, ordered security protocol comparable to the three-step MCP publisher protocol the plan fully specifies. The plan states 'dogfooding the same digest-verify discipline' but does not declare the concrete steps (HTTPS-only curl, verify-before-execute ordering, no pipe-into-shell) for this component, leaving the access posture of a CI-executable download undeclared.
findings.location: Scope section — Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev]; 'actionlint installed via a pinned-version GitHub release download + SHA-256 verification in a Makefile prerequisite'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section under 'Makefile + pyproject.toml [dev]', expand the actionlint install description to mirror the MCP publisher three-step protocol explicitly: (1) `curl -fsSL https://github.com/rhysd/actionlint/releases/download/v<PINNED_VER>/actionlint_<PINNED_VER>_linux_amd64.tar.gz -o actionlint.tar.gz`, (2) `echo '<PINNED_SHA256>  actionlint.tar.gz' | sha256sum -c --strict`, (3) `tar xzf actionlint.tar.gz actionlint && mv actionlint $(GOBIN)/actionlint`. Add an AC bullet requiring this three-step Makefile prerequisite and add a `test_release_workflow_pins.py` assertion that the Makefile contains `sha256sum -c` adjacent to the actionlint download target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an implementation step (or operator-attested AC) that records how the pinned SHA-256 was computed and verified (e.g., 'At pin time, run `curl -fsSL <exact-url> -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz && sha256sum mcp-publisher.tar.gz` and confirm the output matches the hard-coded value in release.yml; record the command output in the commit message or PR description as a rationale comment').
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion or step verifies that the SHA-256 hard-coded in `release.yml` for the MCP publisher actually matches the real downloaded archive at the pinned version. `test_mcp_publisher_pin.py` only tests the verify *logic* (corruption → non-zero exit) using a test copy; it does NOT verify that the hard-coded hash value is correct for the real archive. A typo in the SHA-256 would pass the entire test suite and only fail when a real release is attempted.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — MCP publisher AC; 'tests/unit/test_mcp_publisher_pin.py' scope bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the MCP publisher AC: 'The pinned SHA-256 in release.yml is accompanied by a rationale comment showing the `sha256sum` output from the actual downloaded archive at pin time; the implementer records this output in the PR description [operator-attested].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fourth bullet to the `docs/releasing.md` Pinning section (and the corresponding AC substring check) covering how to update the `actionlint` pin: where to find the new release URL, how to compute the SHA-256, and which Makefile variable to update.
findings.finding: The `actionlint` binary pin (version + SHA-256 hardcoded in a Makefile prerequisite) is a maintain-over-time artifact with no documented update procedure in `docs/releasing.md` or anywhere else the plan calls for.
findings.location: Scope: Makefile section; AC bullet 3 (`actionlint` installed via pinned-version release download); `docs/releasing.md` Pinning section AC (three substrings: `pip-compile --generate-hashes`, `MCP publisher`, `full-SHA`)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC for `docs/releasing.md` to require a fourth substring (e.g. `actionlint`) in the Pinning section, and add prose there mirroring the MCP publisher update procedure: 'To update actionlint: (1) find the new release at github.com/rhysd/actionlint/releases, (2) download the linux_amd64 tarball and run `sha256sum`, (3) update `ACTIONLINT_VERSION` and `ACTIONLINT_SHA256` in `Makefile`.'  Add a corresponding `test_release_workflow_pins.py` substring assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that on `sha256sum -c` failure the workflow logs the actual SHA-256 of the downloaded file (`sha256sum mcp-publisher.tar.gz`) before exiting, so operators can distinguish transient download corruption from a supply-chain event.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what diagnostic state is preserved when the `mcp_verify` sha256sum check fails mid-release (e.g., the downloaded-but-rejected archive). If the job fails, the unverified artifact is discarded with no way for the operator to inspect it, making it hard to distinguish a transient download corruption from a genuine supply-chain event.
findings.location: What section — MCP publisher download/verify steps; AC — MCP publisher AC bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step using `if: failure()` after the sha256sum check that runs `sha256sum mcp-publisher.tar.gz` and uploads it as a `evidence-mcp-failed` artifact, enabling post-hoc inspection without retaining the unverified binary beyond the failed run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `test_mcp_publisher_pin.py` (testing sha256sum's correct operation) and instead extend `test_release_workflow_pins.py` to assert the three-step ordering (curl before sha256sum before tar) in `release.yml`, which tests the rebar-specific wiring rather than a system utility.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_mcp_publisher_pin.py` is specified to 'write a test copy of the pinned archive, corrupt one byte, run the mcp_verify sha256sum -c logic, and assert a non-zero exit.' This is a unit test of `sha256sum`'s own correctness — a POSIX standard utility. The codebase already trusts `sha256sum -c --strict` without a corruption test in `infra/gerrit/install-plugins.sh:41-42` and `.github/workflows/external-integration.yml:90` and `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml:164`. There are zero instances of a test that corrupts a file and verifies `sha256sum` detects it. Testing a well-known system utility's detection of corruption is cargo-cult test coverage with no custom logic to validate.
findings.location: Scope section (tests/unit/test_mcp_publisher_pin.py), AC5
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_release_workflow_pins.py`, assert that in the `mcp_verify` job's steps the `sha256sum -c` step appears between the `curl` download step and the `tar xzf` extraction step — that is the rebar-specific invariant; `sha256sum`'s own correctness is not.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `test.yml` (line 55: `run: make lint`) and the `pre-commit` hook (which also invokes `make lint`) to the Scope's consumer enumeration for the Makefile edit, confirming the path-scoped `zizmor`/`actionlint` invocations do not break either consumer.
findings.finding: test.yml is an unenumerated consumer of `make lint` that will be affected by the Makefile changes, but is not listed in the Scope section.
findings.location: Scope section — Makefile edit; .github/workflows/test.yml line 55
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section under the Makefile bullet, add: 'Consumers of `make lint` that are affected: `.github/workflows/test.yml` (line 55) and `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` (line 303) — both will pick up the new zizmor/actionlint calls; path-scoping to `release.yml` ensures they do not fail on pre-existing out-of-scope issues in other workflows.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm in the Scope section that `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` requires no update (the `push: tags: v*` trigger in `release.yml` is preserved by this plan, keeping the test's assertion valid).
findings.finding: The existing `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` contains an inline comment 'tags stay human-pushable (release.yml v* pushes) -> NO tag lock ruleset' that is load-bearing for that test's assertion. The plan does not change the `push: tags: v*` trigger in release.yml, so this comment remains accurate — but the plan's Scope does not enumerate test_mirror_lock_terraform.py as a file to verify remains unaffected.
findings.location: Scope section; tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py line 42

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
